Step 4: IDENTIFY IMPOSSIBLE COLORS - From this round alone, since exactly 3 of the 5 guessed colors are present, the other 2 are impossible. But we already know orange and white are present, so the two impossible from this round are among {green, yellow, purple} (but which two depends on which one is present). Step 5: IDENTIFY UNKNOWNS - The secret has 5 positions. We now know white and orange are definitely present. Exactly one of {green, yellow, purple} is present. Positional constraints: White cannot be at positions 3 (R6) or 4 (R2,R5). Orange cannot be at position 4 (R5,R6). The third color cannot be at its R6 position (green≠0, yellow≠1, purple≠2). Also from R3, one of white or the third color is locked at its R3 position (white at 2, green at 0, yellow at 1, purple at 4). From R5, one of orange or the third color is locked at its R5 position (orange at 3, green at 0, yellow at 1, purple at 2), but purple at 2 conflicts with R6, so orange at 3 is forced for the purple family. Multiple complete assignments satisfy all constraints across different families; the secret is not uniquely determined.

After Round7, the secret is largely determined. Confirmed present: white, orange, purple, blue. Confirmed absent: red, yellow, green, black. Positions: orange at position 3 (from Round5 and confirmed by Round7), purple at position 4 (from Round3). White and blue fill positions 0,1,2 with the fifth position being a duplicate of one of these four colors. Two consistent solutions remain: (0: white, 1: blue, 2: white/orange/purple duplicate) or (0: blue, 1: white, 2: duplicate). Both satisfy all prior constraints.

After Round 8, the secret is uniquely determined. The secret contains purple (twice), white, blue, orange. Positions: 0: purple, 1: white, 2: blue, 3: orange, 4: purple. All prior rounds' feedback are satisfied perfectly. No ambiguity remains.
